team_name: Pathology pair: Jane Skok + Richard Possemato
opportunity_title: SPURRING PROJECTS TO ADVANCE ENERGY RESEARCH AND KNOWLEDGE SWIFTLY (SPARKS)
rationale: Low lexical overlap; plausible conceptual fit based on general scope.
suggested_specific_aim: Define a focused pilot that ties the opportunity deliverable to an existing departmental strength and a measurable endpoint.
next_steps: Confirm eligibility and scope, assign a lead, draft a one page concept, and map resources and preliminary data needs.
suggested_title: DE-FOA-0003164 — Pathology: work, has, experimental, tools
synopsis: This application would address SPURRING PROJECTS TO ADVANCE ENERGY RESEARCH AND KNOWLEDGE SWIFTLY (SPARKS) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on work, has, experimental, tools and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Richard Possemato: I am an Associate Professor in the Department of Pathology at NYU School of Medicine and Co-Leader of the Cancer Cell Biology Program at the Perlmutter Cancer Center. Our research program is focused on identifying metabolic alterations in cancer and the tumor microenvironment, focusing on triple-negative breast cancer and alterations in the metabolism of transformed and immune cells.; Jane Skok: To uncover how CTCF/CTCFL, cohesin, transcription factors, and nucleosomes generate tunable insulation and state-specific 3D genome folding. We build the tools to see it—from fixed-cell 3D FISH and dual-colour live CRISPR imaging to EpiMethylTag, MethNet, and cluster-based single-molecule nano-NOMe phasing—and apply them to show how drivers like NSD2 reprogram insulated domains and how DNA damage imprints lasting chromatin memory..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in work, has, experimental, tools using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

team_name: Pathology pair: Ran Brosh + Daniel Meruelo
opportunity_title: SPURRING PROJECTS TO ADVANCE ENERGY RESEARCH AND KNOWLEDGE SWIFTLY (SPARKS)
suggested_specific_aim: Define a focused pilot that ties the opportunity deliverable to an existing departmental strength and a measurable endpoint.
next_steps: Confirm eligibility and scope, assign a lead, draft a one page concept, and map resources and preliminary data needs.
suggested_title: DE-FOA-0003164 — Pathology: its, research, develop, basic
synopsis: This application would address SPURRING PROJECTS TO ADVANCE ENERGY RESEARCH AND KNOWLEDGE SWIFTLY (SPARKS) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on its, research, develop, basic and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Daniel Meruelo: Our lab focuses on creating a novel cancer treatment platform using the oncolytic Sindbis viral vector (SV) engineered from Sindbis Virus. In the wild, Sindbis Virus is transmitted by mosquitoes among birds and mammals, which serve as amplifying hosts.; Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in its, research, develop, basic using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

team_name: Pathology team: Jane Skok, Richard Possemato et al
opportunity_title: SPURRING PROJECTS TO ADVANCE ENERGY RESEARCH AND KNOWLEDGE SWIFTLY (SPARKS)

suggested_title: DE-FOA-0003164 — Pathology: work, has, processes, experimental
synopsis: This application would address SPURRING PROJECTS TO ADVANCE ENERGY RESEARCH AND KNOWLEDGE SWIFTLY (SPARKS) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on work, has, processes, experimental and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Richard Possemato: I am an Associate Professor in the Department of Pathology at NYU School of Medicine and Co-Leader of the Cancer Cell Biology Program at the Perlmutter Cancer Center. Our research program is focused on identifying metabolic alterations in cancer and the tumor microenvironment, focusing on triple-negative breast cancer and alterations in the metabolism of transformed and immune cells.; Stefan Feske: Dr. Feske's research interests focus on investigating the role of ion channels and transporters, particularly calcium release-activated calcium (CRAC) channels, in the signaling pathways of immune cell function.; Jane Skok: To uncover how CTCF/CTCFL, cohesin, transcription factors, and nucleosomes generate tunable insulation and state-specific 3D genome folding. We build the tools to see it—from fixed-cell 3D FISH and dual-colour live CRISPR imaging to EpiMethylTag, MethNet, and cluster-based single-molecule nano-NOMe phasing—and apply them to show how drivers like NSD2 reprogram insulated domains and how DNA damage imprints lasting chromatin memory.; Susan Smith: My laboratory studies mechanisms of chromosome cohesion and segregation in human normal cells, stem cells, aging cells, and cancer cells. We focus on how sister chromatid cohesion is established at repetitive sequences including telomeres, ribosomal DNA arrays, and centromeres in S phase during DNA replication and how these sequences are resolved and accurately distributed to daughter cells in mitosis..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in work, has, processes, experimental using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.
